petitioner_background: The petitioner is a chef from China seeking classification as an employment-based immigrant under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act as an alien of extraordinary ability in the arts. He served as executive chef of the Qingdao Hotel in Qingdao, China from 1998 to 2004 and claimed various culinary awards and recognition in both China and the United States. The Sheraton LaGuardia East Hotel in Flushing, New York intended to employ him as a chef, and he later worked part-time at Hong Kong Cuisine in Miami Beach, Florida.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted: (1) a February 2006 'Certificate of Honor' from the Chinese American Gourmet Association (CAGA) indicating he won the 'Gold Medal Award,' supported by a June 14, 2006 letter from the CAGA President stating the 2006 annual competition had over 800 participants and the petitioner won the grand prize; (2) an August 18, 2007 letter from the CAGA president stating CAGA 'is a New York-based organization and one of the most important associations in the Chinese American Community'; (3) a December 10, 1993 'Certificate of Honor' from the 3rd National Competition of Culinary Arts indicating he received the Gold Medal Award; (4) a November 1999 'Certificate of Award' certifying he won the 'Most Outstanding Award at Shandong Culinary Arts Competition (Flour Food Section)'; and (5) an August 8, 2007 letter from the vice president of the China Cuisine Association (CCA) stating CCA was officially established in 1987 and certified by the Chinese government, and that the 3rd National Culinary Art Competitions is 'a national, most prestigious competition in the field' representing 'the highest level of expertise in culinary art in China.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner failed to meet this criterion. Counsel's statement that CAGA 'is a leading organization in North America in the industry of Chinese food/restaurant' was not supported by documentary evidence, and the unsupported assertions of counsel do not constitute evidence (citing Matter of Obaigbena, Matter of Laureano, and Matter of Ramirez-Sanchez). The petitioner submitted no documentation to establish that a Gold Award from a CAGA competition is nationally or internationally recognized as an award of excellence in the culinary arts. Similarly, the petitioner submitted no documentation corroborating the CCA vice president's statements about the 3rd National Culinary Art Competitions, and going on record without supporting documentary evidence is not sufficient (citing Matter of Soffici). The evidence did not establish that awards presented by the CCA or at the Culinary Arts Competition are nationally or internationally recognized as awards of excellence in the petitioner's field of endeavor.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted: (1) a copy of a January 3, 2003 edition of the Qingdao Morning News with an accompanying translation presented as 'an abstract,' where the original document appears to be a recipe for a particular dish by the petitioner; (2) a copy of a July 11, 2006 edition of the China Press with only an abstract translation, which appears to be about the petitioner but the translation does not include the author; and (3) copies of magazine supplements to the World Journal Weekly dated July 2-8, 2006, July 9-15, 2006, and July 16-22, 2006, each apparently including a recipe by the petitioner, with only partial translations that do not identify the author.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner failed to meet this criterion. The translations did not comply with 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), which requires documents submitted in a foreign language to be accompanied by a full English translation rather than abstracts or partial translations. The Qingdao Morning News abstract did not establish that the article was primarily about the petitioner or his work. The China Press and World Journal Weekly translations failed to include the author of the material as required by the regulation. Because the translations did not meet regulatory requirements, the evidence was not probative and was not accorded any weight. Additionally, the AAO noted the petitioner must establish sustained acclaim through extensive documentation, and other than one document in 2003, all of the petitioner's documentation in this category was dated in July 2006, two months prior to the filing date of September 8, 2006. Acclaim garnered during a one-month period cannot be considered sustained.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted: (1) an August 1997 letter from the Chinese Association of Culinary Arts inviting him to serve as a judge at the 'Golden-Cup National Culinary Art Competition'; (2) an August 11, 2007 letter from the president of the Chinese (Gourmet) Associates of Culinary Arts (CACA) stating he served as chief judge of the 1997 Golden-Cup National Culinary Art Competition and that the petitioner served as chief judge of 'noodles and desserts'; and (3) a July 8, 2008 letter from the Vice President of the Shandong Provincial Association of Culinary Art (SPACA) stating the petitioner was invited to serve as judge at the 4th Shandong Provincial Competitions of Culinary Arts in September 2000 and the 5th Shandong Provincial Culinary Arts in December 2004 because of his 'outstanding overall achievements,' and that SPACA 'is the highest professional institute of its kind in Shandong province' authorized to review and evaluate state-owned business establishments in Shandong province.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner failed to meet this criterion. The petitioner submitted no documentation that he actually served as judge in the 2000 and 2004 Shandong Provincial competitions. Further, the petitioner submitted no documentation to corroborate the SPACA vice president's statements regarding the role of SPACA in Shandong province (citing Matter of Soffici). The petitioner must establish sustained acclaim through extensive documentation, and the petitioner's evidence established only that he sat as a judge on one panel in 1997, which was insufficient to meet the criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed to meet this criterion based on the recipes and photographs of finished products that appeared in issues of the Qingdao Morning News, the China Press, and the magazine supplements to the World Journal Weekly.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner failed to meet this criterion. The wording of the criterion indicates it is intended for those in the visual arts such as sculptors and painters. The petitioner submitted no documentation that his work was the subject of any artistic exhibition or showcase.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted: (1) a June 13, 2006 letter from the General Manager of the Qingdao Hotel in Qingdao, China, stating the petitioner served as executive chef from 1998 to 2004 and 'earned a salary of 10,000 RMB per month, the highest ever in our history for chefs'; (2) a January 2006 statement bearing the seal of the Department of Labor of Qingdao City indicating 'prevailing salaries' for chefs ranged from 1,000 RMB for a 3rd class chef to 8,000 RMB for a super 1st class chef; (3) a May 12, 2006 letter from the Executive Chef of the Sheraton LaGuardia East Hotel in Flushing Center, New York, stating the hotel intended to employ the petitioner as a chef at $28 per hour when he received employment authorization; (4) a July 19, 2007 letter from Hong Kong Cuisine in Miami Beach, Florida, verifying the petitioner had been employed part-time (20 hours per week) at $29 per hour; (5) a check dated July 13, 2007 in the amount of $1,089.55 and another unprocessed check dated July 27, 2007; and (6) a page from the Foreign Labor Certification Data Center website accessed on August 8, 2007, indicating chefs and head cooks in the Miami-Miami Beach-Kendall, Florida Metro area could expect a top salary of $24.42 per hour. Counsel asserted the petitioner's salary was more than 20% higher than the lowest amount a chef can expect, and on appeal asserted the petitioner was receiving a salary 11% higher than those at the top level.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner failed to meet this criterion. The documentation was unclear whether a 'super 1st class chef' is the same or equivalent to an 'executive chef,' and the petitioner submitted no documentation of the prevailing salary for chefs outside of Qingdao City or how his salary compared relative to all in his field. The petitioner submitted no documentation that he was actually employed at the time the petition was filed on September 8, 2006, and on his Form G-325A he claimed no employment between December 2004 and July 2006, so the Sheraton's intention to employ him at a specified rate was not evidence of having commanded a high salary in the past. His employment at Hong Kong Cuisine occurred after the filing date, and a visa petition may not be approved based on speculation of future eligibility or after the petitioner becomes eligible under a new set of facts (citing Matter of Michelin Tire Corp. and Matter of Katigbak). Even considering the Hong Kong Cuisine employment, the sole evidence of payment was a single check dated July 13, 2007, and the other check was unprocessed and could not be considered documentary evidence. A single check that post-dates the filing of the petition cannot be considered evidence of a high salary. Additionally, the petitioner's salary comparison was limited to the Miami-Miami Beach-Kendall Metro area, with no documentation comparing his salary to all chefs nationally or internationally.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because review of the record did not establish that the petitioner distinguished himself as a chef to such an extent that he may be said to have achieved sustained national or international acclaim or to be within the small percentage at the very top of his field. The evidence was not persuasive that the petitioner's achievements set him significantly above almost all others in his field. The petitioner failed to meet any of the five criteria he claimed (awards, published material, judging, exhibition, and high salary), and therefore did not establish eligibility pursuant to section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act. The burden of proof in visa petition proceedings remains entirely with the petitioner under Section 291 of the Act, and the petitioner did not sustain that burden.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190 (Reg. Comm. 1972)
Matter of Michelin Tire Corp., 17 I&N Dec. 248 (Reg. Comm. 1978)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural issues with the petitioner's evidence: translations of foreign language documents (Qingdao Morning News, China Press, World Journal Weekly) did not comply with 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3) because they were submitted as abstracts or partial translations rather than full English translations, and failed to identify the author of the materials. As a result, the evidence was not probative and was not accorded any weight. Additionally, multiple letters from association officials were uncorroborated by supporting documentary evidence, and counsel's unsupported assertions were found not to constitute evidence.
